terms.plain: A naming word: a person, place, thing or idea. In Latin, a noun’s ending often changes to show what job it is doing in the sentence.
terms.example: agnus — lamb; ecclesia — church

terms.plain: A doing or being word. A Latin verb can carry information about who is acting, when it happens and whether the subject acts or receives the action.
terms.example: orat — prays; est — is

terms.plain: A small word that shows a relationship such as in, with, from, to or through. In Latin, a preposition normally expects the following noun to use a particular case.
terms.example: in ecclesia — in the church

terms.plain: A describing word. In Latin, an adjective changes its ending so that it matches the noun it describes in gender, number and case.
terms.example: bonus pastor — good shepherd

terms.plain: The matching system between connected words. A Latin adjective normally matches its noun in gender, number and case, even when the two words are separated.
terms.example: The matching endings help show which description belongs to which noun.

terms.plain: Singular means one; plural means more than one. Latin usually shows this difference through word endings.
terms.example: agnus — a lamb; agni — lambs

terms.plain: A way of showing what job a noun or pronoun is doing. English relies heavily on word order; Latin often changes the ending instead. Nominative, accusative, genitive, dative and ablative are the main cases met in this course.
terms.example: agnus can be the doer; agnum can receive the action.

terms.plain: A family of noun-ending patterns. Saying that a noun is first, second or third declension tells you which set of endings it normally follows; it is not a level of difficulty or a separate kind of Latin.
terms.example: Think of it as the noun’s pattern family.

terms.plain: The case most often used for the subject—the person or thing doing the action or being described.
terms.example: Agnus tollit… — The Lamb takes away…

terms.plain: The case most often used for the direct object—the person or thing directly receiving the action. It is also used after certain prepositions.
terms.example: videt agnum — he sees the lamb

terms.plain: The case commonly used for belonging, origin or close connection. English often expresses it with ‘of’ or an apostrophe-s.
terms.example: Agnus Dei — Lamb of God

terms.plain: The case commonly used for the person or thing receiving, benefiting from or being affected by something. English often uses ‘to’ or ‘for’.
terms.example: dat discipulo librum — he gives the student a book

terms.plain: A flexible case used for ideas such as by, with, from, in or through. Context and any nearby preposition tell you which sense is intended.
terms.example: cum amico — with a friend

terms.plain: A small surviving case used mainly to say where something is, especially with names of towns, small islands and a few common nouns.
terms.example: Romae — at Rome

terms.plain: One of Latin’s three grammatical genders: masculine, feminine and neuter. It is a grammatical grouping, not a claim that the thing itself has a biological sex.
terms.example: verbum — word

terms.plain: These labels simply identify who is involved. First person is I or we; second person is you; third person is he, she, it or they.
terms.example: oro — I pray; oras — you pray; orat — he, she or it prays

terms.plain: The part of a verb system that places an action in time or shows how it unfolds. Latin has several past, present and future forms.
terms.example: orat — prays; oravit — prayed or has prayed

terms.plain: A verb form normally used for what is happening now, what regularly happens or what is generally true.
terms.example: orat — he or she prays / is praying

terms.plain: A past form often used for an action that was continuing, repeated or not viewed as a single completed event.
terms.example: orabat — he or she was praying / used to pray

terms.plain: A verb form used for what will happen.
terms.example: orabit — he or she will pray

terms.plain: A past form that normally presents an action as completed. Depending on context, English may translate it as ‘did’ or ‘has done’.
terms.example: oravit — he or she prayed / has prayed

terms.plain: A frightening label for a simple idea: a completed action done by he, she, it or they. ‘Third person’ tells you who; ‘perfect’ tells you the action is viewed as completed.
terms.example: vidit — he, she or it saw; viderunt — they saw

terms.plain: A past-before-the-past form. It describes something that had already happened before another past event.
terms.example: oraverat — he or she had prayed

terms.plain: A form for something that will already have been completed by a future point.
terms.example: oraverit — he or she will have prayed

terms.plain: Voice tells you whether the subject performs the action or receives it. Active: ‘the priest blesses the people’. Passive: ‘the people are blessed’.
terms.example: benedicit — blesses; benedicitur — is blessed

terms.plain: A completed action received by the subject. It is often built from a participle plus a form of ‘to be’.
terms.example: laudatus est — he was praised / has been praised

terms.plain: A verb mood often used when the action is presented as intended, possible, commanded, feared, questioned or dependent on another idea rather than stated as a plain fact. The surrounding words usually reveal why it is there.
terms.example: ut oret — so that he may pray

terms.plain: A subjunctive form that presents an action as completed. Its exact English meaning depends on the clause around it.
terms.example: oraverit — may have prayed / has prayed, depending on context

terms.plain: The form used to tell or ask someone to do something. A prohibition is a negative command—telling someone not to do it. A passive imperative tells someone to receive or undergo an action.
terms.example: ora — pray; noli timere — do not be afraid

terms.plain: A common verb that does not follow the most regular pattern in every form. ‘Irregular’ does not mean random; it means the learner must become familiar with its particular pattern.
terms.example: esse — to be; posse — to be able; ire — to go

terms.plain: A verb that looks passive in form but normally has an active meaning. Treat it as a special pattern rather than trying to translate it as passive.
terms.example: loquitur — he or she speaks

terms.plain: The basic ‘to…’ form of a verb, such as to pray or to see. Latin also has infinitives that indicate time and voice, including a future infinitive for an action expected to happen later.
terms.example: orare — to pray

terms.plain: A verb form used like an adjective. It describes a noun while retaining an idea of action. A future participle describes someone or something about to act or intending to act.
terms.example: orans — praying; oraturus — about to pray

terms.plain: A word used instead of a noun, such as he, she, it, they, this or that. Latin pronouns change form according to their job in the sentence.
terms.example: ille — he / that man; eum — him

terms.plain: A pointing word such as this, that, these or those. In Latin it changes ending to match its number, gender and case.
terms.example: hic — this; ille — that

terms.plain: A part of a sentence beginning with who, which or that and giving more information about a noun.
terms.example: Agnus, qui tollit… — the Lamb, who takes away…

terms.plain: A way of reporting what someone says, thinks, knows or perceives without quoting their exact words. Latin commonly uses an accusative subject plus an infinitive.
terms.example: dicit eum venire — he says that he is coming

terms.plain: A question reported inside another sentence rather than asked directly.
terms.example: rogat quid faciat — he asks what he is doing / should do

terms.plain: A clause explaining why something is done—its aim or purpose. Latin often introduces it with ut or ne and uses a subjunctive verb.
terms.example: venit ut oret — he comes in order to pray

terms.plain: A clause explaining what happened as a consequence. Latin often uses ut with a subjunctive, usually after a word suggesting such, so or so great.
terms.example: tam laetus est ut cantet — he is so happy that he sings

terms.plain: A clause beginning with cum. Depending on context, cum can mean when, since or although. The verb form and the surrounding story help identify the sense.
terms.example: cum veniret — when / since / although he was coming

terms.plain: A compact side phrase, usually containing a noun and participle in the ablative, that gives background such as time, cause or circumstance. It is grammatically ‘loose’ from the main sentence.
terms.example: missa finita — when the Mass had ended

terms.plain: A verbal noun: the action itself treated as a thing. English often uses an -ing form in this way.
terms.example: ars orandi — the art of praying

terms.plain: A verbal adjective usually carrying the sense of something needing or deserving to be done. With a form of ‘to be’, it can express obligation.
terms.example: hoc faciendum est — this must be done

terms.plain: An if–then sentence. Latin changes its verb forms according to whether the condition is presented as factual, possible, unlikely or contrary to fact.
terms.example: si venit, gaudeo — if he comes, I rejoice

terms.plain: The system used for ideas such as good, better and best, or strong, stronger and strongest.
terms.example: bonus, melior, optimus — good, better, best

terms.plain: A verb normally used without a specific person or thing as its subject. English has similar expressions such as ‘it rains’ or ‘it is necessary’.
terms.example: oportet — it is necessary

terms.plain: A subgroup within the third declension whose endings preserve an extra i in certain forms. It is best understood as a variation within the normal third-declension pattern.
terms.example: civis, civium — citizen, of citizens
